canonical: 가는 말이 고와야 오는 말이 곱다
meaning: If the words you send are kind, the words that return will be kind; treat others as you wish to be treated.
origin: Classical Korean proverb (속담); the image is of words traveling out and returning, emphasising reciprocity of speech.

variants.surface: 가는 말이 고와야 오는 말이 곱다

meaning: Dust gathered makes a mountain; many small savings or efforts accumulate into something great.
origin: Korean proverb equivalent to 'many a mickle makes a muckle.' 태산 (泰山) refers to Mount Tai in China, the archetype of great size.

meaning: Fix the barn after the cow has been lost; take action only after the damage is done.
origin: Korean proverb equivalent to 'lock the stable door after the horse has bolted.' Reflects universal human tendency to respond reactively.

meaning: A frog in a well; someone with a narrow, limited worldview.
origin: Derived from the Chinese proverb 井底之蛙 (jǐng dǐ zhī wā — frog at the bottom of a well), itself from Zhuangzi. The Korean form is a standard calque.

meaning: Even a sheet of paper is lighter when two people lift it; cooperation makes any task easier.
origin: Korean proverb emphasising collective effort. 백지장 is a blank sheet of paper — already light — making the point that cooperation helps even with the simplest of tasks.

meaning: Habits formed at age three stay with you until eighty; early habits are lifelong.
origin: Korean proverb equivalent to 'the child is father to the man.' 여든 (eighty) represents extreme old age, emphasising the permanence of early-formed tendencies.

meaning: An empty cart makes the most noise; those with the least substance are the loudest.
origin: Korean proverb parallel to 'empty vessels make the most sound.' The image of a cart with no load rattling loudly is universal.

meaning: Cover your eyes and say peek-a-boo; self-deception that fools no one else.
origin: Korean proverb. 아웅 is an interjection used in the peekaboo game (아웅다웅); the image is of someone who thinks hiding their own eyes makes them invisible.

canonical: 호랑이 없는 골에 토끼가 왕 노릇 한다
meaning: In the absence of the tiger, the rabbit acts as king; minor figures dominate when greater ones are absent.
origin: Korean proverb. The tiger represents authority or true excellence; the rabbit represents opportunistic minor actors who only rise because of the absence of their betters.

variants.surface: 호랑이 없는 골에 토끼가 왕 노릇 한다

canonical: 개구리 올챙이 적 생각 못 한다
meaning: A frog cannot remember being a tadpole; the successful forget their humble origins.
origin: Korean proverb equivalent to 'he has forgotten the rock from which he was hewn.' 올챙이 (tadpole) represents a person's lower origins before transformation.

variants.surface: 개구리 올챙이 적 생각 못 한다

meaning: Even three mal of beads are worthless until strung together; raw material has no value until organized.
origin: Korean proverb. 말 (mal) is a traditional dry-measure unit; 서 말 is a large quantity. The proverb emphasises that knowledge, talent, or resources must be properly deployed to have value.

meaning: Even if the sky collapses, there is a hole to escape through; there is always a way out.
origin: Korean proverb of reassurance; conveys optimism that a solution exists even in the most hopeless-seeming situations.

meaning: Fall seven times, rise eight; perseverance through repeated failure.
origin: Sino-Korean four-character compound (사자성어), calque of the Japanese/Chinese 七転八起 (nana korobi ya oki / qī diān bā qǐ). Widely used in Korean motivational contexts.

meaning: Impartial and selfless; acting without favoritism or personal agenda.
origin: Sino-Korean compound (公平無私); used in formal administrative, legal, and ethical contexts. Classical Chinese origin, 公 (public/fair) + 平 (even) + 無私 (without self-interest).

meaning: One stone, two birds; achieve two goals with one action.
origin: Sino-Korean compound (一石二鳥); direct calque of the Chinese/English 'kill two birds with one stone.' Standard modern Korean.

meaning: Review the old to understand the new; learning from the past illuminates the present.
origin: From the Analects (論語 Lúnyǔ) of Confucius (Chapter 2): '溫故而知新，可以爲師矣' — 'He who reviews the old and learns the new may serve as a teacher.' The Korean form 온고지신 (溫故知新) is the standard Sino-Korean compound.

meaning: Fellow sufferers pity each other; shared misfortune creates empathy.
origin: Sino-Korean compound (同病相憐), from Wu Zixu's speech in the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian): those with the same illness share sympathy. Standard Korean idiom.

meaning: Surrounded on all four sides by Chu songs; completely surrounded, with no way out.
origin: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian): at the Battle of Gaixia (202 BCE), the defeated Xiang Yu heard soldiers all around him singing songs from his home state of Chu, realising his army had defected. The Sino-Korean form 四面楚歌 is a fixed idiom for complete encirclement.

meaning: East wind past the ear of a horse; ignore advice completely; let words go in one ear and out the other.
origin: Sino-Korean compound (馬耳東風), from Du Fu's poem '答王十二寒夜獨酌有懷' (8th c. CE): the east wind blows past the horse's ear without effect. Standard Korean idiom for ignoring advice.

meaning: Four-character set phrase; a Sino-Korean idiomatic compound of four characters.
origin: Korean grammatical/cultural term (四字成語); the broader category name for Sino-Korean four-character idioms (like 一石二鳥, 同病相憐 etc.), equivalent to Chinese 成語 (chéngyǔ).
why_it_matters: Understanding 사자성어 as a category helps learners recognize that these compounds form a distinct register of educated Korean speech. Many derive directly from classical Chinese.
